title: Phase 2: Task Types and List Count
Add Type field to Task model and JSONL serialization
description:
Problem: The Task struct has no Type field, so users cannot classify tasks as bug, feature, task, or chore.

Solution: Add Type string field to Task struct with omitempty JSON serialization, plus validation and normalization functions for the closed set (bug, feature, task, chore).

Outcome: Task struct includes Type field that round-trips through JSON correctly, with validation functions covering all edge cases.

Do:
1. In internal/task/task.go, add Type string with json:"type,omitempty" tag. Add to taskJSON struct. Update MarshalJSON/UnmarshalJSON to copy the field.
2. Add ValidateType(typ string) error -- checks one of bug/feature/task/chore. Empty string valid (optional).
3. Add NormalizeType(typ string) string -- trims whitespace, lowercases.
4. Add ValidateTypeNotEmpty(typ string) error -- for --type flag context, errors on empty/whitespace with message pointing to --clear-type.
5. Tests in internal/task/task_test.go.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Task.Type field with json:"type,omitempty" tag
- MarshalJSON includes type when non-empty, omits when empty
- UnmarshalJSON populates Type from JSON
- ValidateType("") returns nil; valid values return nil; invalid returns error listing allowed values
- NormalizeType("  BUG  ") returns "bug"
- ValidateTypeNotEmpty("") returns error mentioning --clear-type
- Backward compatible: existing tasks without type deserialize with empty Type

Tests:

- "it omits type field when empty"
- "it unmarshals type field from JSON"
- "it unmarshals task without type field (backward compat)"

- "it rejects whitespace-only input on --type flag context"

- Invalid type value: ValidateType returns error with allowed values list
- Whitespace-only: NormalizeType trims to empty, ValidateTypeNotEmpty catches

Outcome: After cache rebuild, every task row has type column populated (NULL when unset). Schema migration via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS on fresh databases. Existing caches rebuilt from JSONL.

2. Update Rebuild INSERT statement to include type column.
3. Handle type value same as description: *string pointer, nil when empty.
4. Tests in internal/storage/cache_test.go.

- Cache.Rebuild inserts type value; empty -> NULL, set -> stored value
- Fresh cache opens correctly with new schema
- Existing test suite passes

- "it stores NULL for empty type after rebuild"

Edge Cases: None -- mechanical schema change.

Context: SQLite cache is expendable, rebuilt from JSONL via SHA256 hash comparison. CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS means fresh databases get new column automatically.

title: Note add subcommand
description:

Solution: Add a note subcommand to App.Run that routes to add and remove sub-subcommands (following the dep add/rm pattern). Implement RunNoteAdd which resolves the task ID, validates the text, appends a new Note with the current timestamp, updates the task's Updated timestamp, and persists via store.Mutate.

Outcome: tick note add <id> <text> appends a note to the specified task. Text is collected from all remaining args after the ID (multi-word support). The task's Updated timestamp is refreshed. Output shows the updated task detail.

    - Parse args: first positional arg is the task ID, remaining positional args (non-flag) are joined with spaces to form the note text

description:

    - Resolve task ID via store.ResolveID (partial ID matching from Phase 1)

      - Find task by resolved full ID
      - Validate index: must be >= 1 and <= len(tasks[i].Notes); error if out of bounds
      - If task has no notes, error "task has no notes to remove"
      - Remove note at position index-1 (convert 1-based to 0-based): tasks[i].Notes = append(tasks[i].Notes[:idx], tasks[i].Notes[idx+1:]...)
      - Set tasks[i].Updated = now
    - Output via outputMutationResult
  - Wire "remove" case in handleNote
- Add tests in internal/cli/note_test.go

Acceptance Criteria:
- tick note remove <id> 1 removes the first note
- tick note remove <id> 3 removes the third note when 3+ notes exist
- Index 0 errors (must be >= 1)
- Negative index errors
- Index exceeding note count errors with descriptive message including the count
- Non-integer index errors with parse failure message
- Task with no notes errors with "task has no notes to remove"
- Task's Updated timestamp refreshed after note remove
- Task not found returns error (via ResolveID error messaging)

Tests:
- "it removes note at index 1"
- "it removes note at last index"
- "it removes middle note preserving order" -- 3 notes, remove index 2, verify notes 1 and 3 remain in order
- "it errors on index 0"
- "it errors on negative index"
- "it errors when index exceeds note count"
- "it errors on non-integer index" -- e.g., "abc"
- "it errors when task has no notes"
- "it errors when task not found"
- "it resolves partial ID for note remove"
- "it updates task Updated timestamp on remove"

Edge Cases:
- Index 0: error "note index must be >= 1"
- Index exceeding note count: error with actual count (e.g., "note index 5 out of range, task has 3 notes")
- Negative index: error (either caught by Atoi if prefixed with -, or by >= 1 check)
- Non-integer index: error from strconv.Atoi
- Task with no notes: special error message distinct from out-of-bounds

Context:
Spec says: "tick note remove <id> <index> -- remove by 1-based position. Note remove index must be >= 1 and <= number of existing notes; out-of-bounds errors."
The remove pattern is straightforward slice manipulation. The 1-based indexing matches user expectations (note 1 is the first note shown in tick show). The index validation must happen inside store.Mutate since the note count could differ from what was displayed.

title: Extract generic deduplication and validation helpers in internal/task
description:
severity: medium
sources: duplication

Problem: DeduplicateTags and DeduplicateRefs in internal/task/ implement identical algorithms (iterate, normalize, skip empties, deduplicate by seen-map, preserve order) differing only in the normalizer function. ValidateTags and ValidateRefs follow the same pattern (early-return on empty, call deduplicate, validate each element, check max count). This is ~60 lines of near-duplicate logic across tags.go and refs.go that risks drift as features evolve.

Solution: Extract a deduplicateStrings(items []string, normalize func(string) string) []string helper. Make DeduplicateTags and DeduplicateRefs thin wrappers. Optionally extract a validateCollection helper for the shared validate pattern.

Outcome: Tags and refs deduplication/validation share a single implementation. Adding a new slice-type field in the future requires only a wrapper, not a copy.

Do:
1. In internal/task/, create (or add to an existing shared file) a deduplicateStrings(items []string, normalize func(string) string) []string function implementing the current dedup algorithm.
2. Rewrite DeduplicateTags as: return deduplicateStrings(tags, NormalizeTag).
3. Rewrite DeduplicateRefs as: return deduplicateStrings(refs, strings.TrimSpace).
4. Verify all existing tests in internal/task/ still pass.
5. Optionally: extract a validateCollection helper that takes the deduplicate function, per-item validator, and max count, then rewrite ValidateTags/ValidateRefs as wrappers.

Acceptance Criteria:
- DeduplicateTags and DeduplicateRefs delegate to a shared helper
- No behavioral change -- all existing tests pass without modification
- The shared helper is unexported (internal implementation detail)

Tests:
- All existing tag and ref deduplication tests pass unchanged
- All existing tag and ref validation tests pass unchanged

title: Extract shared validation helpers for type/tags/refs flags in CLI layer

title: Consolidate ResolveID into a single Query call
description:
severity: medium
sources: architecture

Problem: ResolveID in internal/storage/store.go:272-329 calls s.Query() up to twice -- once for exact full-ID match and once for prefix search fallback. Each Query() call acquires a shared lock, reads the JSONL file, checks cache freshness, then releases the lock. This doubles I/O and lock overhead for every full-ID resolution, and ResolveID is called on nearly every command.

Solution: Refactor ResolveID to perform both the exact match attempt and the prefix search within a single s.Query() call.

Outcome: Every ID resolution uses one lock acquisition and one cache-freshness check instead of two, reducing per-command latency.

Do:
1. In store.go, refactor ResolveID so that the exact-match check and the prefix-search fallback both happen inside a single s.Query(func(db *sql.DB) error { ... }) block.
2. Inside the single callback: attempt exact match first; if not found and prefix length < 6, perform the LIKE query; handle ambiguity and not-found errors as before.
3. Run go test ./internal/storage/ ./internal/cli/ to verify no regressions.

Acceptance Criteria:
- ResolveID makes exactly one s.Query() call regardless of whether it does exact or prefix matching
- All existing ResolveID tests pass unchanged
- Ambiguity and not-found error behavior is preserved

Tests:
- All existing ResolveID/partial-ID tests pass unchanged
- Exact full-ID resolution still works correctly
- Prefix resolution with ambiguity still returns error listing matching IDs

title: Phase 6: Analysis (Cycle 2)
Resolve partial IDs for list --parent
description:
**Problem**: `list --parent` normalizes the ID via `task.NormalizeID()` but never resolves it through `store.ResolveID()`. The raw normalized value is passed directly to SQL (`WHERE id = ?`), so `tick list --parent a3f` fails to find `tick-a3f1b2`. Every other ID-accepting parameter (`create --parent`, `update --parent`, `dep add`, positional args) correctly resolves partial IDs. The specification states partial ID resolution "applies everywhere an ID is accepted: positional args, --parent, --blocked-by, --blocks".

**Solution**: In `RunList`, resolve `filter.Parent` through `store.ResolveID()` before entering the `store.Query()` closure, following the same pattern used in `RunUpdate` and `RunCreate`.

**Outcome**: `tick list --parent a3f` correctly resolves to `tick-a3f1b2` (or errors on ambiguity/not-found), consistent with all other commands.

**Do**:
1. In `internal/cli/list.go`, in the `RunList` function, after `store` is opened (line ~150) and before the `store.Query()` call (line ~166), add partial ID resolution for `filter.Parent`:
   ```go
   if filter.Parent \!= "" {
       filter.Parent, err = store.ResolveID(filter.Parent)
       if err \!= nil {
           return err
       }
   }
   ```
2. Remove the parent-existence check inside the `store.Query()` closure (lines 170-176) since `ResolveID` already validates that the task exists.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- `tick list --parent <partial-id>` resolves the partial ID to a full ID before querying
- Ambiguous partial IDs produce an error listing matching IDs
- Non-existent IDs produce a "not found" error
- Full IDs continue to work as before

**Tests**:
- Test that `list --parent` with a unique prefix resolves correctly and returns children
- Test that `list --parent` with an ambiguous prefix returns an appropriate error
- Test that `list --parent` with a non-matching prefix returns a not-found error

title: Extract query-scan helpers in show.go
ParseRefs should delegate to ValidateRefs
Cache Schema Versioning
Cache Schema Versioning
Phase 1: Schema Version Check and Rebuild
Add schema version constant and store in metadata during rebuild
description:
**Problem**: The SQLite cache has no schema-freshness model. When schemaSQL changes between releases (e.g., new columns), CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silently succeeds on the old schema, leaving the on-disk cache incompatible with the new code. Every CLI command then fails with errors like "no such column: t.type". Users must manually delete cache.db to recover.

**Solution**: Add a const schemaVersion = 1 in cache.go and write it into the metadata table during every Rebuild() call, alongside the existing jsonl_hash row. This gives the cache a persistent schema identity that can be checked on subsequent access.

**Outcome**: After Rebuild() completes, the metadata table contains a schema_version row with value "1". A new exported method SchemaVersion() on *Cache reads this value back, returning (int, error) — returning 0, nil when the row is missing (pre-versioning cache) and the parsed integer otherwise.

**Do**:
- In internal/storage/cache.go, add const schemaVersion = 1 at package level (below the existing schemaSQL const).
- In Rebuild(), after the existing INSERT OR REPLACE INTO metadata for jsonl_hash (around line 215), add an analogous statement: INSERT OR REPLACE INTO metadata (key, value) VALUES ('schema_version', ?) with fmt.Sprintf("%d", schemaVersion) as the value. This goes inside the same transaction, before tx.Commit().
- Add a new exported method on *Cache: func (c *Cache) SchemaVersion() (int, error). It queries SELECT value FROM metadata WHERE key = 'schema_version'. If sql.ErrNoRows, return 0, nil. Otherwise parse the value with strconv.Atoi and return it.
- Add a package-level exported accessor: func CurrentSchemaVersion() int { return schemaVersion } — this lets tests (and later ensureFresh) compare the on-disk version to the compiled-in version without reaching into unexported state.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] const schemaVersion = 1 exists in internal/storage/cache.go
- [ ] Rebuild() stores schema_version in the metadata table inside the same transaction as jsonl_hash
- [ ] SchemaVersion() returns the stored version from the metadata table
- [ ] SchemaVersion() returns 0, nil when the row is missing (no error)
- [ ] CurrentSchemaVersion() returns the compiled-in constant
- [ ] All existing tests in internal/storage/ continue to pass

**Tests**:
- "it stores schema_version in metadata after rebuild" — call Rebuild(), then query SELECT value FROM metadata WHERE key = 'schema_version' and assert it equals "1".
- "it returns current schema version via SchemaVersion()" — call Rebuild(), then call cache.SchemaVersion() and assert it equals 1.
- "it returns 0 when schema_version row is missing" — open a fresh cache (no rebuild), call SchemaVersion(), assert return is 0, nil.
- "it stores schema_version in the same transaction as jsonl_hash" — call Rebuild() with duplicate task IDs (to force transaction rollback), then verify neither schema_version nor jsonl_hash were updated (transaction atomicity).

**Context**:
The spec prescribes a simple integer version, not a schema hash, to avoid false triggers from whitespace changes. The value is stored as a string in the metadata table's value TEXT column and parsed back to int on read. schemaVersion should be incremented whenever schemaSQL changes in the future. The cache is ephemeral — no ALTER TABLE migrations, only full rebuilds.

**Spec Reference**: .workflows/specification/cache-schema-versioning/specification.md -- "Fix: Schema Version Constant", items 1 and 4.

title: Check schema version in ensureFresh and delete-rebuild on mismatch
description:
**Problem**: Even with the schema version stored in metadata (Task 1), nothing checks it. A cache.db created by an older binary still opens successfully via OpenCache() (because CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS succeeds on the old schema), and IsFresh() compares only the JSONL hash — which may match, leaving the incompatible schema in use. The version check must happen before IsFresh() to avoid querying a schema-incompatible cache.

**Solution**: Early in ensureFresh() in store.go, after the cache is opened (lazy init), query the on-disk schema_version via cache.SchemaVersion(). If the returned version does not equal CurrentSchemaVersion() (which covers both a mismatch and a missing row returning 0), close the DB, delete the cache file, reopen a fresh cache, and fall through to the existing IsFresh() / Rebuild() logic. The new cache will have no hash, so IsFresh() returns false, triggering a rebuild that writes both the hash and the correct schema_version.

**Outcome**: When ensureFresh() encounters a cache whose schema_version is missing or differs from the compiled-in constant, it transparently deletes and recreates the cache file. The user never sees an error from a schema-incompatible cache.

**Do**:
- In internal/storage/store.go, in the ensureFresh() method, after the lazy-init block (after s.cache = cache around line 376) and before the fresh, err := s.cache.IsFresh(rawJSONL) call (line 379), insert the schema version check:
  1. Call ver, err := s.cache.SchemaVersion().
  2. If err \!= nil, treat it as corruption: log warning, close cache, delete file, reopen (same pattern as the existing IsFresh error handler).
  3. If ver \!= CurrentSchemaVersion(), log a verbose message like "schema version mismatch: got %d, want %d", close cache (s.cache.Close()), set s.cache = nil, call s.removeCache(), reopen via OpenCache(s.cachePath), and assign to s.cache. The fresh empty cache will have no hash, so the existing IsFresh() block will detect staleness and call Rebuild().
- Add a verbose log message (s.verbose(...)) when a schema version mismatch triggers the delete-rebuild path, for observability.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] ensureFresh() queries schema_version before calling IsFresh()
- [ ] Cache with wrong schema version triggers close + delete + reopen (and subsequent rebuild)
- [ ] Cache with missing schema_version row (pre-versioning cache.db) triggers close + delete + reopen (and subsequent rebuild)
- [ ] Cache with correct schema version is preserved — no unnecessary delete or rebuild (beyond normal hash-based freshness)
- [ ] Errors from SchemaVersion() are handled gracefully (delete + recreate, same as existing corruption handling)
- [ ] All existing tests in internal/storage/ continue to pass

**Tests**:
- "it deletes and rebuilds cache when schema_version is wrong" — set up a store with a valid cache, then directly update the metadata row to schema_version = 999 using raw SQL. Trigger Query(). Assert: the cache file was deleted and recreated, the task data is correct, and schema_version now equals CurrentSchemaVersion().
- "it deletes and rebuilds cache when schema_version row is missing" — set up a store with a valid cache, then directly delete the schema_version row from metadata using raw SQL. Trigger Query(). Assert: rebuild happened, tasks are correct, schema_version now exists and equals CurrentSchemaVersion().
- "it preserves cache when schema_version matches" — set up a store, trigger a Query() to prime the cache, trigger another Query(). Assert: no "schema version mismatch" message. Use WithVerbose to capture logs.
- "it handles SchemaVersion query error gracefully" — corrupt the metadata table, then trigger Query(). Assert: recovery succeeds, tasks are queryable, schema_version is correct.

**Edge Cases**:
- Missing schema_version row (pre-versioning cache.db): SchemaVersion() returns 0, which does not equal schemaVersion (1), so the delete-rebuild path triggers. This is the primary upgrade scenario the bug fix targets.
- New empty cache extra rebuild cycle: When a brand-new cache.db is created by OpenCache(), it has no schema_version row yet. SchemaVersion() returns 0, triggering delete + reopen + rebuild. The spec explicitly acknowledges this extra cycle as acceptable since cache creation is infrequent.

**Context**:
The spec states: "Version check happens before IsFresh() to avoid querying a schema-incompatible cache." This ordering is critical. The spec also notes: "On a new cache the missing version triggers the same delete+rebuild path; the extra cycle is acceptable since cache creation is infrequent."

**Spec Reference**: .workflows/specification/cache-schema-versioning/specification.md -- "Fix: Schema Version Constant", items 2, 3, and "Key design decisions" bullets 1 and 4.

title: End-to-end query success after version-triggered rebuild
description:
**Problem**: Tasks 1 and 2 implement the schema version mechanism and the check in ensureFresh(), but we need to verify the complete user-facing scenario: a cache.db produced by an older binary (with an outdated or missing schema version) is transparently replaced, and subsequent CLI-level queries against the store return correct results without error. This is the acceptance test that proves the bug is fully fixed.

**Solution**: Write integration-level tests in store_test.go that simulate the real upgrade scenario: create a store with tasks, build the cache, tamper with the cache to simulate a pre-versioning or old-version state (including altering the actual schema to remove a column, replicating the original bug), then verify that Query() succeeds and returns correct data.

**Outcome**: Two end-to-end tests prove that the full flow -- from opening a store with an incompatible cache through to executing a query that references all current columns -- works without error or data loss.

**Do**:
- In internal/storage/store_test.go, add a new test function TestStoreSchemaVersionUpgrade with the following subtests:
  1. "it recovers from pre-versioning cache with missing column and serves correct query results":
     - Create a store with tasks (including a task with a type field set, since type was the column whose absence triggered the original bug).
     - Prime the cache via Query().
     - Close the store.
     - Directly open the cache.db with raw sql.Open, drop the schema_version row from metadata, and drop+recreate the tasks table without the type column, re-inserting task data without the type field. This simulates an old-schema cache.db. Close the raw DB connection.
     - Reopen the store via NewStore().
     - Call store.Query() with a query that selects t.type from tasks t (the exact query pattern that previously failed).
     - Assert: no error, the type value is correct for the task.
  2. "it handles sequential queries after version-triggered rebuild without re-triggering rebuild":
     - Create a store with tasks, prime cache, tamper with schema_version to 0 (simulating missing version).
     - Call store.Query() — this triggers the version-mismatch rebuild.
     - Call store.Query() again immediately.
     - Use WithVerbose to capture log messages. Assert: the first query logs "schema version mismatch" and "rebuilding cache", the second query logs "cache is fresh" with no version mismatch or rebuild messages.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] Test proves that a cache.db with a missing column (simulating old schema) is transparently replaced and a query referencing that column succeeds
- [ ] Test proves that after a version-triggered rebuild, subsequent queries do not re-trigger the rebuild (no extra cycle on second access)
- [ ] Test proves that task data is correct after the rebuild (no data loss from the delete-rebuild cycle)
- [ ] All existing tests in internal/storage/ continue to pass

**Tests**:
- "it recovers from pre-versioning cache with missing column and serves correct query results" — full scenario as described in Do step 1.
- "it handles sequential queries after version-triggered rebuild without re-triggering rebuild" — full scenario as described in Do step 2.

**Context**:
The original bug manifested as "no such column: t.type" when querying after an upgrade that added the type column to schemaSQL. The end-to-end test should replicate this exact failure mode to prove the fix works. The test tampers with the on-disk SQLite file directly (not through the Cache API) to simulate what a real user would have after upgrading their binary.

**Spec Reference**: .workflows/specification/cache-schema-versioning/specification.md -- "Testing requirements" bullet 4: "After version-triggered rebuild, queries succeed normally."

title: Auto-Cascade Parent Status
Phase 1: StateMachine Core with Migration
Create StateMachine struct with Transition method
description:
Problem: Transition logic currently lives as a package-level function task.Transition() in transition.go. The spec requires consolidating all 11 rules into a StateMachine struct. The first step is creating the struct and migrating the existing transition logic (Rule 1) into it as a method.

Solution: Create a new file internal/task/state_machine.go with a stateless StateMachine struct and a Transition() method that absorbs the existing task.Transition() logic. The existing task.Transition() function becomes a thin wrapper delegating to StateMachine.Transition() to avoid breaking callers until task auto-cascade-parent-status-1-6 updates them.

Outcome: StateMachine.Transition() passes all existing transition tests with identical behavior. The existing task.Transition() still works via delegation. No regressions.

Do:
- Create internal/task/state_machine.go with type StateMachine struct{} (stateless, no constructor needed)
- Move the transition table (transitionTable map), statusIn helper, and the transition logic into StateMachine.Transition(t *Task, action string) (TransitionResult, error) -- same signature and behavior as existing task.Transition()
- Keep TransitionResult in its current location (it's a shared type)
- Convert the existing task.Transition() function in transition.go into a thin wrapper: func Transition(t *Task, command string) (TransitionResult, error) { var sm StateMachine; return sm.Transition(t, command) }
- Create internal/task/state_machine_test.go with tests calling StateMachine.Transition() directly, covering: all 7 valid transitions, all 9 invalid transitions, unknown command error, closed timestamp set/cleared, updated timestamp changes, and no-modification-on-error guarantee

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] StateMachine struct exists in internal/task/state_machine.go as a stateless struct (no fields)

title: Migrate ValidateAddDep into StateMachine
description:
Problem: Dependency validation logic (ValidateDependency, ValidateDependencies) lives as package-level functions in dependency.go. The spec requires consolidating this into the StateMachine as ValidateAddDep(), implementing Rules 10 (cycle detection) and 11 (child-blocked-by-parent rejection).

title: Add ValidateAddChild for cancelled parent guard
description:
Problem: There is currently no validation when adding a child to a parent task. Rule 7 requires blocking the addition of a child to a cancelled parent with an explicit error message directing the user to reopen the parent first.

Solution: Add a ValidateAddChild(parent *Task) error method to StateMachine that returns an error if the parent's status is StatusCancelled, and nil otherwise. This is pure validation -- it does not handle the Rule 6 done-parent reopen (that is the caller's responsibility, addressed in Phase 2/3).

Outcome: sm.ValidateAddChild() blocks adding children to cancelled parents with the specified error message and allows all other parent statuses.

Do:
- Add func (sm *StateMachine) ValidateAddChild(parent *Task) error to state_machine.go
- If parent.Status == StatusCancelled, return fmt.Errorf("cannot add child to cancelled task, reopen it first")
- Otherwise return nil
- Add tests in state_machine_test.go covering: cancelled parent returns error, open/in_progress/done parents return nil

- [ ] Open parent returns nil
- [ ] In-progress parent returns nil
- [ ] Done parent returns nil (Rule 6 reopen is caller's responsibility, not validation)
- [ ] go test ./internal/task/... passes

- "it allows adding child to in_progress parent"

title: Add cancelled dependency guard to ValidateAddDep
description:
Problem: Currently ValidateAddDep checks for cycles (Rule 10) and child-blocked-by-parent (Rule 11), but does not check whether the blocker task is cancelled. Rule 8 requires blocking the addition of a dependency on a cancelled task.

Solution: Add a cancelled-status check to StateMachine.ValidateAddDep() that looks up the blocker task by ID and returns an error if it is cancelled. This check runs before the existing cycle detection and child-blocked-by-parent checks.

Outcome: sm.ValidateAddDep() rejects adding a dependency on a cancelled task with the specified error message while preserving all existing validation behavior.

Do:
- In StateMachine.ValidateAddDep() in state_machine.go, after normalizing IDs but before existing checks, find the blocker task in the tasks slice by matching NormalizeID(tasks[i].ID) == blockerID
- If the blocker task has Status == StatusCancelled, return fmt.Errorf("cannot add dependency on cancelled task, reopen it first")
- If the blocker task is not found in the slice, skip this check
- Add tests in state_machine_test.go covering: cancelled blocker returns error, open/in_progress/done blockers pass through

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Adding a dependency where the blocker is cancelled returns error: "cannot add dependency on cancelled task, reopen it first"
- [ ] Adding a dependency where the blocker is open, in_progress, or done does not trigger this error

- [ ] go test ./internal/task/... passes

- "it allows adding dependency on done task"

Context: Rule 8: Cannot add a dependency on a cancelled task. Error: cannot add dependency on cancelled task, reopen it first. The check should be early in ValidateAddDep so users get the most actionable error message first.

title: Add reopen-under-cancelled-parent guard to Transition
description:
Problem: Currently StateMachine.Transition() only validates the standard transition table (Rule 1). Rule 9 requires an additional guard: reopening a task whose direct parent is cancelled must be blocked with an error directing the user to reopen the parent first.

Solution: Extend StateMachine.Transition() to accept an optional tasks slice context so it can check the direct parent's status when action is "reopen". If the task has a parent and that parent is cancelled, return an error before applying the transition. This keeps Rule 9 inside the Transition method consistent with the spec API surface (no separate ValidateReopen method).

Outcome: Attempting to reopen a task whose direct parent is cancelled returns an error. No new public method is added to StateMachine.

Do:
- Add a TransitionContext struct or add a tasks []Task parameter to Transition: func (sm *StateMachine) Transition(t *Task, action string, tasks []Task) (TransitionResult, error)
- When action == "reopen" and t.Parent is non-empty, look up the parent in tasks; if parent Status == StatusCancelled, return fmt.Errorf("cannot reopen task under cancelled parent, reopen parent first")
- Only check the direct parent, not the full ancestor chain (spec Rule 9 says "cancelled parent")
- Existing callers that don't need the parent check pass nil or empty slice for tasks
- Add tests in state_machine_test.go

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Transition() with action "reopen" checks direct parent status
- [ ] Task with cancelled direct parent returns error: "cannot reopen task under cancelled parent, reopen parent first"
- [ ] Task with no parent returns nil (proceeds normally)
- [ ] Task with open/in_progress/done direct parent proceeds normally
- [ ] No separate ValidateReopen method on StateMachine (consistent with spec API surface)
- [ ] go test ./internal/task/... passes

title: Implement Cascades for upward start cascade (Rule 2)
description:
Problem: When a child transitions to in_progress, all open ancestors should automatically become in_progress. Currently no cascade logic exists.

Solution: Implement cascade computation for Rule 2 in StateMachine.Cascades() method in internal/task/cascades.go. Given a changed task and action, if action is "start", walk up the parent chain and emit CascadeChange for each ancestor that is open, setting it to in_progress.

Outcome: Cascades() returns []CascadeChange list that, for a start action, includes all open ancestors. The function is pure -- it does not mutate any tasks.

- Create internal/task/cascades.go with Cascades method on StateMachine
- Define CascadeChange struct: Task *Task, Action string, OldStatus Status, NewStatus Status
- For action == "start": build map of task ID to *Task, walk up via Parent field, for each open ancestor append CascadeChange
- For other actions, return nil for now

Acceptance Criteria:

- [ ] Ancestors already in_progress are skipped
- [ ] Ancestors in terminal states stop the chain
- [ ] Deeply nested chains (5+ levels) produce correct cascade entries
- [ ] Non-start actions return nil/empty
- [ ] Cascades() does not mutate any task

title: Implement Cascades for downward done/cancel cascade (Rule 4)
description:
Problem: When a parent is marked done or cancelled, all non-terminal children (and their descendants) should copy the parent's terminal status.

Solution: Extend Cascades() in internal/task/cascades.go to handle done and cancel actions on tasks that have children. Walk downward: find all direct children that are non-terminal, emit CascadeChange entries, then recurse into their children.

Outcome: Cascades() returns downward cascade entries for done/cancel actions, covering all non-terminal descendants. Terminal children are left untouched.

Do:
- Extend Cascades() method for action == "done" and action == "cancel"
- Build parent-to-children map from tasks slice
- For each non-terminal child, append CascadeChange with same action as parent
- Recursively process each cascaded child's children (BFS/iterative queue)
- Children already terminal are skipped
- Dependency state does not gate cascade (advisory dependency principle)

Acceptance Criteria:

- [ ] Cascades() for "cancel" returns entries for all non-terminal descendants with cancelled status

- [ ] Children with unresolved dependencies are still cascaded
- [ ] Cascades() does not mutate any task

title: Implement Cascades for upward completion cascade (Rule 3)
description:
Problem: When all children of a parent reach terminal states, the parent should auto-complete: done if at least one child is done, cancelled if all children are cancelled.

Solution: Extend Cascades() to handle the completion check after a task reaches a terminal state. Look at the changed task's parent and check if all siblings are now terminal. If so, determine the parent's new status and emit a CascadeChange.

Outcome: Cascades() returns an upward completion cascade entry for the parent when all children are terminal. The correct terminal status is chosen based on whether any child is done.

- Only cascade if parent is non-terminal

title: Implement Cascades for reopen under done parent (Rule 5)

Problem: When a child is reopened and its parent is done, the parent must also reopen to open. This applies recursively up the ancestor chain. Cancelled ancestors block the chain.

- If cancelled, open, or in_progress, stop walking

title: Implement ApplyWithCascades with queue-based processing
description:
Problem: Individual cascade rules produce []CascadeChange lists, but these need to be applied in sequence with each applied change potentially triggering further cascades. A queue-based processor with deduplication is needed.

Solution: Implement ApplyWithCascades() on StateMachine. It applies the primary transition via Transition(), then enters a loop: compute cascades, enqueue, dequeue one at a time, apply via Transition(), record Transition history entry (Auto: true), compute further cascades, repeat until queue drains. Seen-map prevents duplicate processing.

Outcome: ApplyWithCascades() returns primary TransitionResult, full []CascadeChange, and error. All tasks mutated in-place. Each cascaded task has Transition history entry with Auto: true. Primary task gets Auto: false.

Do:
- Create internal/task/apply_cascades.go with ApplyWithCascades method
- Signature: func (sm *StateMachine) ApplyWithCascades(tasks []Task, target *Task, action string) (TransitionResult, []CascadeChange, error)
- Step 1: Call sm.Transition(target, action, tasks). On error, return immediately.
- Step 2: Append Transition{From: result.OldStatus, To: result.NewStatus, At: target.Updated, Auto: false} to target.Transitions
- Step 3: Call sm.Cascades(tasks, target, action) for initial cascade list
- Step 4: Initialize queue with initial cascades, seen-map with target.ID pre-seeded, results slice
- Step 5: Loop while queue non-empty: dequeue, check seen, mark seen, call sm.Transition(change.Task, change.Action, tasks), append history entry with Auto: true, append to results, call Cascades for further cascades, enqueue new
- Step 6: Return primary result, results slice, nil

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Primary transition applied and returns correct TransitionResult
- [ ] Primary task gets Transition history entry with Auto: false
- [ ] Each cascaded task gets Transition history entry with Auto: true
- [ ] Multi-level cascades chain correctly
- [ ] Seen-map prevents processing same task twice
- [ ] Empty cascade list returns empty []CascadeChange
- [ ] Invalid primary transition returns error with no side effects
- [ ] Queue drains completely -- no infinite loops

Tests:
- "it applies primary transition and returns result"
- "it records transition history on primary task"
- "it applies single-level upward start cascade"

- "it chains upward completion after downward cascade"

- "it returns error for invalid primary transition"
- "it handles reopen cascade chain"
- "it records auto transitions on all cascaded tasks"

title: Wire ApplyWithCascades into RunTransition
description:
Problem: RunTransition currently calls task.Transition() on a single task. It does not compute or display cascades.

Solution: Replace task.Transition() call with StateMachine.ApplyWithCascades(). When cascades produced, build CascadeResult and render via FormatCascadeTransition. When no cascades, use FormatTransition for backward-compat output. All changes atomic in single Store.Mutate.

Outcome: tick start/done/cancel/reopen automatically compute and display cascades. Non-cascade transitions look identical to before.

Do:
- In RunTransition, create task.StateMachine{}, call sm.ApplyWithCascades inside store.Mutate
- If len(cascades) == 0: use FormatTransition (no visual change)
- If len(cascades) > 0: build CascadeResult by populating Cascaded from []CascadeChange and Unchanged by scanning tasks for children of the primary task that are in terminal states (done/cancelled) and were not included in the cascade changes (already terminal before the cascade)
- Quiet mode: print only task ID
- Return modified tasks slice from Mutate

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] RunTransition calls ApplyWithCascades instead of task.Transition
- [ ] All mutations atomic in single Store.Mutate
- [ ] Empty cascades uses FormatTransition (identical to current)
- [ ] Non-empty cascades uses FormatCascadeTransition
- [ ] Unchanged terminal children populated by scanning for children already in terminal state not in cascade list
- [ ] Quiet mode prints only task ID
- [ ] All existing transition tests pass

Tests:
- "it transitions single task with no cascades using FormatTransition"
- "it renders cascade output when done triggers downward cascade"
- "it renders cascade output when start triggers upward cascade"
- "it persists all changes atomically"
- "it includes unchanged terminal children in cascade output"
- "it suppresses output in quiet mode"
- "it returns error for task not found"
- "it returns error for invalid transition"

Spec Reference: .workflows/specification/auto-cascade-parent-status/specification.md -- Architecture: Store Integration

title: Wire ValidateAddChild and done-parent reopen cascade into RunCreate
description:
Problem: RunCreate does not validate parent status before adding a child. A child can be added to a cancelled parent (should be blocked) and adding to a done parent does not trigger reopen cascade.

Solution: After resolving parent ID inside Store.Mutate, call ValidateAddChild() to block cancelled parent (Rule 7). If parent is done, call ApplyWithCascades to reopen it (Rule 6). Display cascade after created task detail.

Outcome: Creating with --parent on cancelled parent returns error. Creating with --parent on done parent triggers automatic reopen cascade.

Do:
- In create.go inside store.Mutate, after parent resolution: call sm.ValidateAddChild(parentTask)
- If parent is done: call sm.ApplyWithCascades(tasks, parentTask, "reopen") for Rule 6
- Append new task after validation/reopen
- After Mutate: output created task detail, then cascade output if any

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Cancelled parent returns error: "cannot add child to cancelled task, reopen it first"
- [ ] Done parent triggers reopen to open
- [ ] Reopen cascade propagates upward to done ancestors (Rule 5)
- [ ] Created task detail output first, then cascade output
- [ ] Open/in_progress parent produces no cascade
- [ ] All changes atomic in one Mutate call
- [ ] Existing create tests pass

Tests:
- "it blocks adding child to cancelled parent"
- "it reopens done parent when adding child"
- "it cascades reopen to done grandparent"
- "it does not cascade when parent is open"
- "it does not cascade when parent is in_progress"
- "it persists new task and reopened parent atomically"
- "it displays cascade output after created task detail"

Spec Reference: .workflows/specification/auto-cascade-parent-status/specification.md -- Rule 6, Rule 7

title: Wire reparenting cascade logic into RunUpdate
description:
Problem: RunUpdate changes the Parent field but does not evaluate cascade rules. Reparenting away should trigger Rule 3 on original parent. Reparenting to done parent should trigger Rule 6. Reparenting to cancelled parent should be blocked by Rule 7.

Solution: Inside RunUpdate's Store.Mutate, when opts.parent is provided: validate new parent via ValidateAddChild (Rule 7), if done trigger reopen cascade (Rule 6), after updating parent field evaluate Rule 3 on original parent. Display cascade results.

Outcome: Reparenting correctly enforces Rule 7, triggers Rule 6, and evaluates Rule 3. All changes atomic.

Do:
- Capture original parent ID before updating
- If new parent not empty: ValidateAddChild (blocks cancelled), if done call ApplyWithCascades reopen (Rule 6)
- Update parent field
- If origParent \!= "" and changed: evaluate Rule 3 on original parent -- gather all remaining children of original parent, check if all are terminal (done or cancelled). If so, determine action: if at least one child is done, use "done"; if all children are cancelled, use "cancel". Call ApplyWithCascades(tasks, originalParent, action) to transition and cascade upward.
- After Mutate: output updated task detail, then cascade output

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Reparenting to cancelled parent returns error
- [ ] Reparenting to done parent triggers reopen cascade (Rule 6)
- [ ] Reparenting away triggers Rule 3 re-evaluation on original parent
- [ ] If all remaining children terminal and at least one is done, original parent auto-completes to done
- [ ] If all remaining children cancelled (none done), original parent auto-completes to cancelled
- [ ] Mixed done and cancelled remaining children: original parent auto-completes to done
- [ ] Non-terminal remaining children prevent auto-completion
- [ ] Clearing parent (--parent "") triggers Rule 3 on original parent
- [ ] All changes atomic in single Mutate
- [ ] Existing update tests pass

Tests:
- "it blocks reparenting to cancelled parent"
- "it reopens done parent when reparenting to it"
- "it triggers Rule 3 on original parent when reparenting away"
- "it triggers Rule 3 with cancelled result when all remaining children cancelled"
- "it triggers Rule 3 with done result when remaining children are mix of done and cancelled"
- "it does not trigger Rule 3 when original parent still has non-terminal children"
- "it handles clearing parent"
- "it handles reparent to done parent plus Rule 3 on original"

Spec Reference: .workflows/specification/auto-cascade-parent-status/specification.md -- Reparenting note, Rules 3, 6, 7

title: Phase 4: Analysis (Cycle 1)
Pretty format cascade tree rendering
description:
**Problem**: The spec explicitly shows pretty format rendering cascades as a nested tree with box-drawing characters (grandchildren indented under parent children), but the implementation renders all cascade entries as a flat list at the same indentation level. Additionally, unchanged terminal entries are only collected for direct children, not deeper descendants. The CascadeEntry type lacks parent/hierarchy information, making nested rendering impossible with the current data model.

**Solution**: Add a Parent field to CascadeEntry (or add hierarchy information to CascadeResult), then update PrettyFormatter.FormatCascadeTransition to render nested tree output matching the spec examples. Also update buildCascadeResult to collect unchanged terminal entries recursively for all descendants, not just direct children. JSON format remains flat per spec.

**Outcome**: Pretty format cascade output matches the spec's nested tree structure with proper box-drawing indentation for grandchildren and deeper descendants. Unchanged terminal descendants at all levels appear in the output.

**Do**:
1. Add a `ParentID` field to `CascadeEntry` in `internal/cli/format.go` to carry hierarchy information
2. Update `buildCascadeResult` in `internal/cli/transition.go` to populate `ParentID` on each cascade entry and to collect unchanged terminal entries recursively (all descendants, not just direct children)
3. Update `PrettyFormatter.FormatCascadeTransition` in `internal/cli/pretty_formatter.go` to build a tree from the flat entries using `ParentID`, then render with nested box-drawing characters matching the spec examples
4. Verify ToonFormatter and JSONFormatter remain unaffected (flat output)

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Pretty format downward cascade output shows grandchildren indented under their parent child entries with nested box-drawing characters
- Pretty format upward cascade output renders correctly for multi-level ancestor chains
- Unchanged terminal descendants at all hierarchy levels appear in the output
- Toon format remains flat with `(auto)` and `(unchanged)` markers
- JSON format remains a flat array structure

**Tests**:
- Test pretty format downward cascade with 3-level hierarchy (parent -> children -> grandchildren) matches spec example
- Test pretty format upward cascade with grandparent chain matches spec example
- Test unchanged terminal grandchildren appear in pretty output
- Test toon and JSON formats unchanged by the refactor

title: Move Rule 9 out of Transition into ApplyWithCascades
description:
**Problem**: The spec defines `Transition(t *Task, action string)` but the implementation uses `Transition(t *Task, action string, tasks []Task)` because Rule 9 (block reopen under cancelled parent) needs the full task list. This leaks a contextual concern into the core transition method. The backward-compatible wrapper `task.Transition()` passes nil for tasks, silently skipping Rule 9 validation -- a correctness trap for any caller not using ApplyWithCascades.

**Solution**: Move Rule 9 checking into `ApplyWithCascades` (which already has the task list) and restore `Transition` to its original two-parameter signature `(t *Task, action string)`. Rule 9 is a contextual pre-condition like cascade rules, not an intrinsic status transition rule.

**Outcome**: Transition signature matches the spec. Rule 9 is enforced consistently for all callers going through ApplyWithCascades. No nil-tasks escape hatch exists.

**Do**:
1. In `internal/task/state_machine.go`, remove the `tasks []Task` parameter from the `Transition` method on `StateMachine`
2. Move the Rule 9 check (block reopen under cancelled parent) into `ApplyWithCascades`, before calling `Transition`
3. Remove or update the backward-compatible `Transition` wrapper in `internal/task/transition.go` so it no longer needs to pass nil
4. Update all call sites that pass tasks to `Transition` directly
5. Verify Rule 9 is still enforced via ApplyWithCascades in all CLI commands that perform transitions

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- `StateMachine.Transition` accepts only `(t *Task, action string)` matching the spec
- Rule 9 (block reopen under cancelled parent) is enforced in ApplyWithCascades
- No caller can silently skip Rule 9 by using a different entry point
- All existing transition tests pass

**Tests**:
- Test that reopening a task under a cancelled parent returns error when going through ApplyWithCascades
- Test that Transition alone does not check parent status (it is a pure transition validator)
- Test backward-compatible wrapper still works for simple transitions

title: Extract Rule 3 completion evaluation into shared function
description:
**Problem**: `evaluateRule3()` in `internal/cli/update.go` re-implements the "are all children terminal? done vs cancelled?" detection logic that already lives in `cascadeUpwardCompletion` in `internal/task/cascades.go`. Both build the same allTerminal/anyDone boolean pattern, determine the action string, and derive the new status. If Rule 3 criteria ever change, two places need updating.

**Solution**: Extract the "should this parent auto-complete and with what action" evaluation into a shared function in the task package (e.g., `EvaluateParentCompletion(tasks []Task, parentID string) (action string, shouldComplete bool)`). Both `cascadeUpwardCompletion` and `evaluateRule3` call it.

**Outcome**: Rule 3 completion detection logic exists in exactly one place in `internal/task/`. Changes to Rule 3 criteria only require one update.

**Do**:
1. Add `EvaluateParentCompletion(tasks []Task, parentID string) (action string, shouldComplete bool)` to `internal/task/cascades.go` (or a new file in the task package)
2. Refactor `cascadeUpwardCompletion` to call `EvaluateParentCompletion` instead of inline child-scanning
3. Refactor `evaluateRule3` in `internal/cli/update.go` to call `EvaluateParentCompletion` instead of reimplementing the logic
4. Ensure both callers handle the result identically to their current behavior

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Child-scanning logic for Rule 3 exists in one place only
- cascadeUpwardCompletion behavior unchanged
- evaluateRule3 / reparenting behavior unchanged
- All existing tests pass

**Tests**:
- Test EvaluateParentCompletion with all children done returns ("done", true)
- Test EvaluateParentCompletion with all children cancelled returns ("cancel", true)
- Test EvaluateParentCompletion with mixed terminal children returns ("done", true)
- Test EvaluateParentCompletion with non-terminal children returns ("", false)
- Test reparenting still triggers Rule 3 on original parent

title: Extract cascade output helper function
description:
**Problem**: The same conditional pattern for rendering cascade output appears 4 times across 3 files (`transition.go`, `create.go`, `update.go`): check if cascades slice is empty, call FormatTransition for simple output or buildCascadeResult + FormatCascadeTransition for cascade output. Each instance is 5-7 lines with identical structure differing only in variable names.

**Solution**: Extract a helper function like `outputTransitionOrCascade(stdout io.Writer, fmtr Formatter, id, title string, result TransitionResult, cascades []CascadeChange, tasks []Task) error` that encapsulates the conditional. All four call sites reduce to a single function call.

**Outcome**: Cascade output rendering logic exists in one place. Adding new commands that trigger cascades requires one function call instead of duplicating the pattern.

**Do**:
1. Add `outputTransitionOrCascade` function in `internal/cli/transition.go` (or a shared helpers file in the cli package)
2. Replace the 4 duplicate blocks in `transition.go:56-63`, `create.go:293-298`, `update.go:447-453`, and `update.go:457-463` with calls to the new helper
3. Verify output is identical before and after

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- All 4 call sites use the extracted helper
- No duplicate cascade output pattern remains
- CLI output is identical for all transition/create/update commands

**Tests**:
- Existing CLI integration tests for transition, create, and update commands pass unchanged
- Test the helper directly with empty cascades (should call FormatTransition)
- Test the helper with non-empty cascades (should call FormatCascadeTransition)

title: Extract parent validation and reopen helper
description:
**Problem**: Both `RunCreate` and `RunUpdate` contain nearly identical 15-18 line blocks that iterate tasks to find the parent by ID, call `ValidateAddChild` to enforce Rule 7, check if parent status is done, and call `ApplyWithCascades` with "reopen" to trigger Rule 6. The only differences are variable names.

**Solution**: Extract a function like `validateAndReopenParent(tasks []Task, parentID string, sm *StateMachine) (TransitionResult, []CascadeChange, bool, error)` that encapsulates the validate-then-reopen pattern.

**Outcome**: Parent validation and Rule 6 reopen logic exists in one place. Both create.go and update.go call it.

**Do**:
1. Add `validateAndReopenParent` function in a shared location in the cli package (e.g., `internal/cli/parent_helpers.go` or in an existing shared file)
2. Replace the duplicate blocks in `create.go:228-246` and `update.go:338-357` with calls to the new function
3. Return values should indicate whether a reopen occurred and provide the transition result and cascade changes for output rendering

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Both RunCreate and RunUpdate use the extracted helper
- Rule 7 (block add child to cancelled parent) still enforced
- Rule 6 (reopen done parent on new child) still triggered
- No behavioral change in create or update commands

**Tests**:
- Existing tests for creating a task under a done parent pass unchanged
- Existing tests for reparenting to a done parent pass unchanged
- Existing tests for creating/reparenting to a cancelled parent (error) pass unchanged

title: Defensive copy of task data for cascade display output
description:
**Problem**: The `allTasks` variable is set to the `tasks` slice from inside the `Mutate` callback, then used after `Mutate` returns to build cascade display output. The CLI layer depends on an implementation detail of how Mutate manages its buffer lifecycle. If `Store.Mutate` ever reuses or clears the slice buffer, display code reads stale/corrupt data. Currently safe but fragile coupling.

**Solution**: Copy the display-relevant data inside the Mutate closure into dedicated output structs, rather than holding a reference to the mutable tasks slice. The CascadeChange structs already contain task pointers, so at minimum copy the unchanged children data needed for display.

**Outcome**: Display output code does not depend on Mutate's internal buffer lifecycle. Safe against future Store implementation changes.

**Do**:
1. In `internal/cli/transition.go`, `create.go`, and `update.go`, identify the data needed for display output after Mutate returns
2. Inside the Mutate closure, copy the needed data (task IDs, titles, statuses for unchanged children) into local structs rather than capturing the full tasks slice reference
3. Update `buildCascadeResult` to work with the copied data instead of the full tasks slice
4. Verify all cascade display output remains identical

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- No reference to the Mutate callback's tasks slice is held after Mutate returns
- Display output is built from copied data
- All existing CLI output tests pass unchanged

**Tests**:
- Existing transition, create, and update tests with cascades pass unchanged
- Verify output correctness for cascades with unchanged terminal children

title: Unknown Flags Silently Ignored
Phase 1: Flag Validation and Normalizations
Phase 2: Parser Cleanup and Regression Verification
Normalize dep rm to dep remove and remove --from=value syntax
description:
Problem: Two prerequisite normalizations must be applied before the central flag validator can be built. (1) dep rm is inconsistent with note remove and the top-level remove command. (2) parseMigrateArgs supports --from=value equals-sign syntax, which is a special case the central validator would need to handle.

Solution: Rename dep rm to dep remove in the handleDep switch and update RunDepRm to RunDepRemove. Remove the --from=value case from parseMigrateArgs. Update all tests referencing dep rm to use dep remove. Add a test that dep rm now returns an unknown sub-command error.

Outcome: dep rm is renamed to dep remove consistently throughout the codebase, --from=value equals-sign syntax is removed from parseMigrateArgs, and all tests are updated accordingly.

Do:
1. In internal/cli/dep.go: In handleDep, rename case "rm": to case "remove":\. Update error message in default case and usage from <add|rm> to <add|remove>. Rename RunDepRm to RunDepRemove. In parseDepArgs, the subCmd parameter will now be "remove" so usage string updates automatically.
2. In internal/cli/migrate.go: In parseMigrateArgs, remove the case strings.HasPrefix(args[i], "--from="): branch entirely.
3. In internal/cli/dep_test.go: Update all runDep(t, dir, "rm", ...) calls to runDep(t, dir, "remove", ...). Rename TestDepRm to TestDepRemove and TestDepRmPartialID to TestDepRemovePartialID. Add TestDepRmReturnsError that runs dep rm and asserts exit code 1 with stderr containing unknown dep sub-command 'rm'.
4. In internal/cli/migrate_test.go: Find any tests using --from=value syntax and update them to --from value, or add a test asserting --from=beads is no longer accepted.
5. In internal/cli/help.go: Update the dep command entry: change Usage from tick dep <add|rm> to tick dep <add|remove>.
6. Run go test ./internal/cli/... to confirm all tests pass.

Acceptance Criteria:
- tick dep remove tick-aaa tick-bbb removes the dependency (same behaviour as old dep rm)
- tick dep rm tick-aaa tick-bbb returns exit code 1 with error containing "unknown dep sub-command 'rm'"
- tick migrate --from=beads fails (equals-sign syntax no longer supported)
- tick migrate --from beads still works (space-separated)
- All existing dep tests pass with updated sub-command name
- Help text for dep command shows <add|remove> not <add|rm>

Tests:
- "it removes dependency via dep remove"
- "it returns unknown sub-command error for dep rm"
- "it accepts --from value (space-separated) on migrate"
- "it rejects --from=value (equals-sign) on migrate"
- "it shows <add|remove> in dep help text"

Edge Cases:
- Existing tests referencing dep rm: All must be updated to dep remove. Search the entire test suite for "rm" in dep-related contexts.
- --from= with empty value: After removing equals-sign support, parseMigrateArgs will not find a --from value and return the "required" error.

Spec Reference: specification.md - "Normalize Dep Subcommand" and "Normalize Migrate Flag Parsing" sections.

title: Reproduce bug and build flag metadata with central validator
description:
Problem: Unknown flags like --blocks on dep add are silently ignored. No mechanism to detect unknown flags because each command parser skips args starting with - that it doesn't recognise. A central validator is needed with flag metadata from each command.

Solution: Write a failing test reproducing the exact dep add --blocks scenario. Create a CommandFlags type mapping command names to valid flags (with boolean vs value-taking distinction), a ValidateFlags function checking args against that set, and a commandFlags registry covering every command.

Outcome: A ValidateFlags function and commandFlags registry exist that can detect unknown flags on any command, with correct handling of boolean vs value-taking flags, global flag pass-through, and two-level command error formatting.

Do:
1. Create internal/cli/flags.go with:
   - FlagDef struct: type FlagDef struct { TakesValue bool }
   - CommandFlags type: type CommandFlags map[string]map[string]FlagDef
   - commandFlags variable populated with all 20 commands from spec's Command Flag Inventory:
     init: empty, create: --priority(v), --description(v), --blocked-by(v), --blocks(v), --parent(v), --type(v), --tags(v), --refs(v)
     update: --title(v), --description(v), --priority(v), --parent(v), --clear-description(b), --type(v), --clear-type(b), --tags(v), --clear-tags(b), --refs(v), --clear-refs(b), --blocks(v)
     list: --ready(b), --blocked(b), --status(v), --priority(v), --parent(v), --type(v), --tag(v), --count(v)
     show, start, done, cancel, reopen: empty
     ready: same as list minus --ready
     blocked: same as list minus --blocked
     dep add, dep remove, note add, note remove: empty
     remove: --force(b), -f(b)
     stats, doctor, rebuild: empty
     migrate: --from(v), --dry-run(b), --pending-only(b)
   - ValidateFlags(command string, args []string, flags CommandFlags) error:
     Iterates args. For each arg starting with -: check if global flag (--quiet, -q, --verbose, -v, --toon, --pretty, --json, --help, -h), if yes skip. Look up in flags[command], if not found return fmt.Errorf with format: unknown flag "%s" for "%s". Run 'tick help %s' for usage. (using helpCommand for the help ref). If found and TakesValue, skip next arg.
   - helpCommand helper: for two-level commands (contains space), return parent; otherwise return command itself.

2. Create internal/cli/flags_test.go with unit tests (see Tests section).

Acceptance Criteria:
- ValidateFlags returns nil for valid flag combinations on every command
- ValidateFlags returns error with exact format: unknown flag "{flag}" for "{command}". Run 'tick help {helpCmd}' for usage.
- Value-taking flags correctly cause next argument to be skipped
- Boolean flags do not cause next argument to be skipped
- Global flags (--quiet, -q, --verbose, -v, --toon, --pretty, --json, --help, -h) accepted on any command
- Short flags like -x rejected; -f accepted on remove
- Two-level commands use fully-qualified name in error, parent in help reference
- commandFlags map covers all 20 commands

Tests:
- "it returns nil for args with no flags"
- "it returns nil for args with known command flags"
- "it returns error for unknown flag"
- "it returns error for unknown flag on dep add (bug repro)"
- "it skips global flags without error"
- "it skips value after value-taking flag"
- "it does not skip value after boolean flag"
- "it rejects short unknown flags"
- "it accepts -f on remove"
- "it uses parent command in help hint for two-level commands"
- "it returns error for flag on command with no flags"
- "it handles global flags interspersed with command args"

Edge Cases:
- Value-taking flags consuming next arg: --priority 3 --unknown must report --unknown, not 3
- Short aliases: -f only valid on remove; -q, -v, -h are global
- Two-level command error format: helpCommand splits on space, returns first token
- Commands with empty flag sets: any non-global flag must be rejected

Spec Reference: specification.md - "Flag Metadata", "Command Flag Inventory", "Error Behavior", "Two-Level Commands" sections.

title: Wire validation into parseArgs and both dispatch paths
description:
Problem: The ValidateFlags function exists (from Task 2) but is not called anywhere. Unknown flags are still silently ignored because parseArgs skips them pre-subcommand and command handlers skip them post-subcommand.

Solution: Modify parseArgs to return an error when it encounters an unknown flag before the subcommand. In App.Run, call ValidateFlags on rest after subcommand is known but before dispatching. Cover both dispatch paths: main switch (post-format-resolution) and doctor/migrate path (pre-format-resolution). Exclude version and help.

Outcome: Every command (except version and help) validates flags through the central validator before handler invocation, covering both the main dispatch switch and the doctor/migrate dispatch path.

Do:
1. In internal/cli/app.go:
   - Change parseArgs signature from func parseArgs(args []string) (globalFlags, string, []string) to func parseArgs(args []string) (globalFlags, string, []string, error).
   - In the \!foundCmd block where strings.HasPrefix(arg, "-") currently skips unknown flags, replace with: return flags, "", nil, fmt.Errorf("unknown flag %q. Run 'tick help' for usage.", arg).
   - Update App.Run call site: flags, subcmd, subArgs, err := parseArgs(args[1:]). If err \!= nil, write fmt.Fprintf(a.Stderr, "Error: %s\n", err) and return 1.
   - After version and help early returns, before doctor/migrate dispatch:
     For doctor: call ValidateFlags("doctor", subArgs, commandFlags). If error, write to stderr and return 1.
     For migrate: call ValidateFlags("migrate", subArgs, commandFlags). If error, write to stderr and return 1.
   - After NewFormatConfig but before main switch: determine fully-qualified command name. For dep and note, peek into subArgs for sub-subcommand. Call ValidateFlags(qualifiedCmd, restArgs, commandFlags). If error, write to stderr and return 1.
   - Do NOT validate for version or help.

2. Update all parseArgs call sites in tests (internal/cli/cli_test.go):
   - Find all occurrences of parseArgs( in cli_test.go (currently 4 call sites at lines 345, 357, 370, 386). Update each from flags, subcmd, _ := parseArgs(...) or flags, subcmd, subArgs := parseArgs(...) to include the new error return: flags, subcmd, _, err := parseArgs(...) / flags, subcmd, subArgs, err := parseArgs(...). Assert err is nil in each case.

3. Create integration tests in internal/cli/flags_test.go or flag_validation_test.go (see Tests section).

Acceptance Criteria:
- parseArgs returns error for unknown flags before the subcommand
- Pre-subcommand error format: unknown flag "{flag}". Run 'tick help' for usage.
- Post-subcommand unknown flags produce: unknown flag "{flag}" for "{command}". Run 'tick help {helpCmd}' for usage.
- version and help commands excluded from flag validation
- Both dispatch paths (main switch and doctor/migrate) validate flags
- Two-level commands validate against correct sub-subcommand flag set
- The dep add --blocks bug report scenario is tested and rejected
- All existing tests pass (parseArgs call sites updated)

Tests:
- "it rejects unknown flag on dep add via full dispatch"
- "it rejects unknown flag before subcommand"
- "it does not validate flags for version command"
- "it does not validate flags for help command"
- "it rejects unknown flag on doctor"
- "it rejects unknown flag on migrate"
- "it rejects unknown flag on list"
- "it rejects unknown flag on create"
- "it accepts known flags on create through dispatch"

Edge Cases:
- Pre-subcommand unknown flag error: different format from post-subcommand (no command name)
- help/version bypass: help accepts --all and command names. version ignores everything. Neither triggers validation.
- doctor/migrate dispatch path: dispatched before format resolution in separate code path
- Two-level commands with unknown sub-subcommand: skip validation, let handleDep produce its own error
- ready/blocked commands: internally prepend --ready/--blocked. Validator validates against ready/blocked flag sets which exclude those flags.

Spec Reference: specification.md - "Flow", "Pre-Subcommand Validation", "Dispatch Paths", "Two-Level Commands", "Excluded Commands", "Error Behavior" sections.

title: Validate global flag pass-through and value-taking flag skipping
description:
Problem: While Tasks 2-3 built and wired the validator, comprehensive verification is needed that global flags pass through correctly in all positions and that value-taking flags properly skip their argument across the full command surface. The ready and blocked commands have a subtle interaction where --ready on tick ready or --blocked on tick blocked should be rejected.

Solution: Write a comprehensive test suite exercising global flag pass-through, value-taking flag skipping on commands with many value flags (like create and update), and the ready/blocked edge cases. Test every command that has no flags rejects any unknown flag. Test every command that has flags accepts them all.

Outcome: Comprehensive test coverage confirms that global flags pass through on all commands, value-taking flags correctly skip their argument, ready rejects --ready, blocked rejects --blocked, and the original bug report scenario is verified end-to-end.

Do:
1. Create or extend internal/cli/flag_validation_test.go with table-driven test:
   - TestFlagValidationAllCommands with subtests per command. For each: test "accepts all valid flags" and "rejects unknown flag".
   - Use ValidateFlags directly (unit-level, no store needed):
     create accepts all 8 flags, update accepts all 12 flags, list accepts all 8 flags
     ready accepts list flags minus --ready; ready rejects --ready
     blocked accepts list flags minus --blocked; blocked rejects --blocked
     remove accepts --force and -f
     migrate accepts --from, --dry-run, --pending-only
     For each no-flag command (show, start, done, cancel, reopen, stats, doctor, rebuild, init, dep add, dep remove, note add, note remove): --unknown is rejected

2. Test global flags interspersed with command args:
   - "global flags are accepted on any command" -- test --quiet, -q, --verbose, -v, --toon, --pretty, --json, --help, -h on commands like create, list, show, dep add
   - "global flags mixed with command flags" -- ValidateFlags("create", []string{"--json", "title", "--priority", "1", "--verbose"}, commandFlags) returns nil

3. Test value-taking flag skipping edge cases:
   - "value that looks like a flag is skipped" -- ValidateFlags("create", []string{"--description", "--not-a-flag"}, commandFlags) returns nil
   - "consecutive value-taking flags work correctly" -- ValidateFlags("create", []string{"--priority", "1", "--description", "desc", "--type", "bug"}, commandFlags) returns nil

title: Derive ready/blocked flag sets programmatically from list
description:
**Problem**: The commandFlags registry in flags.go defines the "ready" entry (7 flags) and "blocked" entry (7 flags) by copy-pasting 6 of the 8 flags from the "list" entry, minus --ready and --blocked respectively. The specification explicitly defines the relationship as "ready = list flags minus --ready" and "blocked = list flags minus --blocked". The current implementation encodes this relationship by duplicating the literal flag definitions rather than expressing the derivation. If a new filter flag is added to list, both ready and blocked must be updated independently, creating a three-way sync risk.

**Solution**: Compute the ready and blocked flag sets programmatically from the list entry using an init() function. Copy list's flag map, then delete the excluded flag for each derived command.

**Outcome**: The mathematical relationship (ready = list - {--ready}, blocked = list - {--blocked}) is expressed in code and self-maintaining. Adding a filter flag to list automatically includes it in ready and blocked.

**Do**:
1. In flags.go, remove the literal "ready" and "blocked" entries from the commandFlags map literal.
2. Add an init() function that: (a) copies the list entry's map into a new map for "ready", deleting "--ready"; (b) copies the list entry's map into a new map for "blocked", deleting "--blocked"; (c) assigns both to commandFlags.
3. Write a small helper function (e.g., copyFlagsExcept(source map[string]FlagDef, exclude string) map[string]FlagDef) that creates a shallow copy of the flag map and deletes the excluded key.
4. Verify the existing tests TestFlagValidationAllCommands (which checks flag counts: ready=7, blocked=7) and TestReadyRejectsReady / TestBlockedRejectsBlocked still pass.
5. Run go test ./internal/cli/ -count=1.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- The commandFlags map literal contains no "ready" or "blocked" entries
- An init() function derives ready and blocked from list's flags
- commandFlags["ready"] has exactly 7 entries (list's 8 minus --ready)
- commandFlags["blocked"] has exactly 7 entries (list's 8 minus --blocked)
- go test ./internal/cli/ -count=1 passes

**Tests**:
- TestFlagValidationAllCommands verifies flag counts (ready=7, blocked=7) and that all valid flags are accepted
- TestReadyRejectsReady verifies --ready is rejected on ready command
- TestBlockedRejectsBlocked verifies --blocked is rejected on blocked command
- Run go test ./internal/cli/ -count=1 -- all pass

title: Add drift-detection test between commandFlags and help registry
description:
**Problem**: Two independent registries define flag information for the same commands: commandFlags in flags.go maps commands to their valid flags with type metadata, and commands in help.go lists flags with display metadata (name, arg hint, description). These must be kept in sync manually. Adding a flag to commandFlags without updating help.go means help text is incomplete. Adding a flag to help.go without updating commandFlags means the validator rejects a documented flag. There is no compile-time or test-time check ensuring the two registries agree.

**Solution**: Add a test that iterates commandFlags entries and verifies each non-global flag name appears in the corresponding commandInfo.Flags slice, and vice versa. This is a lightweight guard that catches drift without restructuring the registries.

**Outcome**: Any future flag addition that updates one registry but not the other is caught by a failing test, preventing silent drift between validation and documentation.

**Do**:
1. In flag_validation_test.go (or flags_test.go -- whichever is the canonical location for ValidateFlags unit tests after Task 1), add a new test function TestCommandFlagsMatchHelp.
2. For each entry in commandFlags, look up the corresponding commandInfo by name using findCommand(). Note: for two-level commands like "dep add", the help registry uses the parent name ("dep") which has no flags listed -- skip two-level commands in this check, or handle them by checking the parent's help entry. The help registry groups "dep" and "note" as single entries without per-subcommand flags, so skip commands containing a space.
3. For each single-level command with flags in commandFlags, extract the flag names from commandFlags[cmd] (excluding short flags like "-f" which are combined with long flags in help.go as "--force, -f").
4. Extract flag names from the commandInfo.Flags slice (note: help.go uses "--force, -f" format in the Name field -- split on ", " to get individual flag names).
5. Assert that the set of long flags in commandFlags matches the set of flag names in commandInfo.Flags. Allow the help entry to contain combined short forms (e.g., "--force, -f") where commandFlags has separate "--force" and "-f" entries.
6. Also check the reverse: flags in help.go should all appear in commandFlags.
7. Run go test ./internal/cli/ -count=1 and verify the new test passes (confirming the registries are currently in sync).

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- A test exists that fails if a flag is added to commandFlags but not to the corresponding help.go commandInfo.Flags (or vice versa)
- The test currently passes, confirming the two registries are in sync
- Two-level commands (dep add, dep remove, note add, note remove) are handled appropriately (either skipped or matched against the parent command)
- Short flag aliases (e.g., -f) are handled correctly in the comparison
- go test ./internal/cli/ -count=1 passes

**Tests**:
- TestCommandFlagsMatchHelp passes on the current codebase
- Manually verify by temporarily adding a flag to commandFlags["list"] without updating help.go -- the test should fail (then revert)

title: Rule6 Parent Reopen Auto Flag
Phase 1: Fix auto flag on system-initiated transitions
Refactor ApplyWithCascades into user/system wrappers with failing test
description:
**Problem**: ApplyWithCascades in internal/task/apply_cascades.go hardcodes Auto: false on the primary target's TransitionRecord (line 43). This is correct for user-initiated transitions but incorrect for system-initiated callers (validateAndReopenParent, evaluateRule3). There is no way to distinguish the two cases because the auto value is not parameterized.

**Solution**: Add an auto bool parameter to ApplyWithCascades and make it unexported (applyWithCascades). Expose two public wrappers: ApplyUserTransition (passes auto=false) and ApplySystemTransition (passes auto=true). Write tests first that verify the auto flag behavior of each wrapper, then implement the refactoring to make them pass. Migrate all 18 existing ApplyWithCascades tests to call ApplyUserTransition with no assertion changes.

**Outcome**: The task package exposes ApplyUserTransition and ApplySystemTransition as the only public entry points for cascading transitions. applyWithCascades is unexported. All 18 existing tests pass unchanged under ApplyUserTransition. Two new tests verify the auto flag distinction between the wrappers.

**Do**:
1. In internal/task/apply_cascades.go, write the new ApplyUserTransition and ApplySystemTransition methods on StateMachine that will wrap the existing logic. Start by adding stub methods that just call ApplyWithCascades — these will compile but the new test for ApplySystemTransition will fail because Auto is still hardcoded to false.
2. In internal/task/apply_cascades_test.go, add two new test cases inside a new TestStateMachine_ApplyUserTransition test function:
   - "it records auto=false on primary target for user transition" — calls ApplyUserTransition on a simple parent+child start scenario, asserts primary target's last TransitionRecord.Auto == false.
   - "it records auto=true on primary target for system transition" — calls ApplySystemTransition on a done parent being reopened, asserts primary target's last TransitionRecord.Auto == true.
3. In internal/task/apply_cascades_test.go, rename the test function TestStateMachine_ApplyWithCascades to TestStateMachine_ApplyUserTransition and change all 18 sm.ApplyWithCascades(...) calls to sm.ApplyUserTransition(...). No assertion changes needed.
4. Now implement the actual refactoring in internal/task/apply_cascades.go:
   - Rename ApplyWithCascades to applyWithCascades (unexported) and add auto bool as a fourth parameter.
   - Change line 43 (Auto: false) to Auto: auto so the primary target's TransitionRecord uses the parameterized value.
   - Add ApplyUserTransition(tasks []Task, target *Task, action string) that calls applyWithCascades(tasks, target, action, false).
   - Add ApplySystemTransition(tasks []Task, target *Task, action string) that calls applyWithCascades(tasks, target, action, true).
   - Update doc comments.
5. Run go test ./internal/task/ — all 20 tests (18 migrated + 2 new) should pass.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] ApplyWithCascades is unexported as applyWithCascades with signature (sm StateMachine) applyWithCascades(tasks []Task, target *Task, action string, auto bool) (TransitionResult, []CascadeChange, error)
- [ ] ApplyUserTransition is exported with signature (sm StateMachine) ApplyUserTransition(tasks []Task, target *Task, action string) (TransitionResult, []CascadeChange, error)
- [ ] ApplySystemTransition is exported with signature (sm StateMachine) ApplySystemTransition(tasks []Task, target *Task, action string) (TransitionResult, []CascadeChange, error)
- [ ] All 18 existing subtests pass unchanged under ApplyUserTransition
- [ ] New test verifies ApplyUserTransition records Auto: false on primary target
- [ ] New test verifies ApplySystemTransition records Auto: true on primary target
- [ ] Cascade transitions still record Auto: true regardless of which wrapper is used
- [ ] go test ./internal/task/ passes with zero failures

**Tests**:
- "it records auto=false on primary target for user transition"
- "it records auto=true on primary target for system transition"
- All 18 existing subtests run under ApplyUserTransition with identical assertions

     - Assert parent.Transitions[0].From == task.StatusDone.
